sentences.text: My name?
Pearl Sapoznik.
The spelling.
P-E-A-R-L S-A-- Sapoznik.
S-A-P-O-Z-N-I-K.
Pirl Sztejnberg.
Yeah.
P-I-R-L S-Z-T-E-- it's a J.
J-N-B-E-R-G.
Sztejnberg.
No.
No Hebrew name.
Just Pirl.
Well, when I went to school, they used to call me Pola.
Yes.
I was born June 12, 1920.
I'm 76 proud.
I was born in this town named Rowne.
R-O-V-N-O, Rovno.
This was not far from the-- from the Russian border, about 60 kilometers from the Russian border.
Poland.
Well, Rowne was a beautiful-- it wasn't a shtetl.
It was a, a [YIDDISH], a big town.
We had about 45,000 people living.
Mostly Jewish people.
The rest were Poles, German, Czech, Ukraine, all kinds.
It was a, a big-- like a-- like a mansion.
It's a big Jewish town.
You have a lot of-- a lot of schools, private schools, gymnasiums, sport clubs, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] stores.
Mostly the-- the stores were-- were in Jewish stores.
Yes.
I remember stores from that were selling like textiles, dishes.
It has a-- you know, like a stock market, you know, that you got $1, you got to the [POLISH].
You change this for-- for-- for zlotys.
It was 9-- 9-- that was a long time ago. 9 zlotys was $1.
When you get a few dollars from America, you go to the [POLISH] and they change it for zlotys.
Yeah.
It was a river that's just crossing the town-- Ossetia.
The river was Ossetia, yeah, a river.
Yeah.
Oh, sure.
I lived on 150 [POLISH] Pontio Twskego.
Well, I'll-- I'll try.
Pontio Twskego.
P-O-N-T-I-O.
Pontio.
T-W-S-K-E-G-O, Pontio Twskego.
I think I came close.
Came close.
My home?
We lived in a small house, about two families.
And it was a small-- an old house.
Not exactly a new house.
An old house.
It had about two rooms and a kitchen space.
No.
No, we have to go to the pump to bring some water.
And it was very, very hard because my father, he used to bring the water.
Yeah.
And we have like a-- a [YIDDISH], you call it in Yiddish.
It's a barrel.
And the barrel has to be filled up with water.
So you go-- he went once in a-- you know, every-- every other day, he went to bring some fresh water.
Yeah, my father.
My father was Isaac, Isaac Steinberg.
My father was a self-educated man, very worldly man.
He knew the history and the geography of-- of the whole world.
And we really looked up to-- to my father.
He was our father, our leader, our everything.
Self.
It was self.
He was reading a lot.
I remember that every week my father used to get the newspaper from Warsaw, like 'Der Moment,' the [POLISH], the and my father was reading those papers.
And on a Saturday afternoon, my father had a lot of friends that used to come.
My mother put some [? kichel ?] on the table, and my father was reading to them.
Everybody was sitting and listening to my father.
They really, really looked up to my father.
He was a-- a house painter.
It was a poor life.
We had a poor life.
Yeah.
Well, the-- the one thing that-- it's really because of my father I survived, I-- I'm alive.
Without my father, I would never escape.
My father took us out, and he said, you're-- you're going to go now.
We're going to leave this place because it was very, very bad.
Yeah.
My mother's name was Rivke.
Git.
G-I-T.
No, my mother took care of the family.
We were a family of three children.
I was the oldest of three girls.
My middle sister, Ruchel, and my younger sister, Pesie.
And my mother took care of the children.
And she went shopping, she cooked, she cleaned, she washed the clothes, she did everything.
My mother never worked.
My mother was a [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
My middle sister, Ruchel, she was younger six years.
No, excuse me.
She was younger two years.
I was two years old when my mother had Ruchel.
And then we have another sister, Pesie.
She was eight years younger than I am.
I remember only my-- my zaide-- my mother's father.
He lived with us all his life.
His wife died.
She was very young.
She left him with five children.
My mother-- four boys and my mother was the only girl.
And my mother took care on the-- on the children.
She raised them all.
My-- my grandfather's name was Icchok.
Icchok.
[NON-ENGLISH SPEECH]
Yeah.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My zaide was my first teacher.
I was about three years old.
And you already-- I have to say brucha.
And I have to say, Shema Yisrael.
And then he used to put a piece of paper and put it in pencil.
You know, alef, bet, gimel.
And I have to-- I have to go over this with-- with a pen.
My zaide was my first teacher.
Yeah.
My first language was Yiddish.
That's all.
We speak only Yiddish.
We speak-- we spoke Polish.
Yeah, you know, with children, to go to school.
We spoke Polish.
Yeah.
I went to school.
At about seven years old, you go to school, to public school.
[POLISH]-- that's School Number Seven, yeah, a public school.
Yeah, I have a favorite subject.
As I start getting the higher grades, I was very good in Polish history.
I know the Polish history.
Everything from-- from the beginning, you know, from the-- all the kings, and the queens, and the novelty, and the nobility.
So that's what I remember.
I was very good in-- in Polish history.
And then I was good in-- like you say it, you used to say, [NON-ENGLISH].
Over here, we would say it, science-- geography, poetry.
That-- that-- I was pretty good in school.
Yeah.
Oh, I have a lot of friends.
Oh yeah, friends.
I started with them to school.
We all live on the same street.
Oh, sure.
My best friend was Sura Steindel.
She was my best friend.
We grew up together.
Then I had another friend, Zisel Heutmann.
Those were-- were my very good friends.
Well, when you're young, you know, you go to their house, or they come to your house.
Not much to do, like on Shabbos.
Not much to do.
Just go to the house, and sit, and-- and talk, and-- it's children.
Have fun.
Yeah.
Yeah, as I got older, I was about 12.
So I belonged to a Zionist organization.
They have a-- a girl.
She was probably a little older than me.
And she made a [NON-ENGLISH] from about 10 children.
And we belonged to Gordonia.
Her name was-- her name was Nesia Lapides.
And when I was in Israel about-- later then, about 40 years later, I met her.
Oh, yeah.
I recognized her brother.
A brother-- the brother said, come.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH] So I went to see her.
That was something.
Yeah.
I was about 12 years old at that time.
But then as I got older, you know, you have more friends.
I said, oh, why do you have to go to Gordonia?
Let's go to [NON-ENGLISH].
So I became a [NON-ENGLISH].
It was also nice, you know.
Every-- all the Zionist organizations was really something look forward to that.
Yeah.
And then my last organization was [NON-ENGLISH].
And that-- that was it.
It was like a [NON-ENGLISH].
You know, like not too left and not too right.
Just in the middle.
The [NON-ENGLISH].
Now it's called [NON-ENGLISH].
But for my time, it was [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah, yeah, they were-- yeah.
I have a lot of friends.
They used to come to me, like for-- they had then a sefer.
They used to come to my house.
I used to go to their house.
And I had-- I had a very busy life.
Yeah.
Sort of.
Yes.
I had-- I had a rebbe coming to teach.
And then I had a Yiddish [YIDDISH] who used to come here.
I didn't go to like a beit yaakov school or a beit rivka.
That was my-- that was my Jewish education, yes.
I have a rebbe was teaching me davening, and then I have [YIDDISH] in Yiddish.
It was a Jewish community.
Everybody was the same.
We were all not rich.
Everybody had the same-- it was close.
It's come Shabbos, everybody go to shul.
And then after shul, you come home, you have your lunch, and your family come to visit you.
Especially my-- the family.
My mother had four brothers, and they used to come every Shabbos and sit and talk.
Reminiscing about the good old days for the Tsar, when the Tsar was-- when the Tsar was the Tsar, yeah.
[NON-ENGLISH].
I don't remember the rabbi's name.
No, I don't.
It was a shamash and a rabbi.
He-- he was doing both, both those things.
Yeah.
Oh yeah, Shabbos.
You know, my father-- as-- as a young girl, I used to go with my father to shul.
My father was a cohen.
And I-- you know, I was about six years old.
He used to take me by his hand.
And I went with my father to shul.
And my father, when he was duchening, I didn't-- I didn't know what is this.
But then I realized that my father was-- yeah.
Coming home, my mother served lunch.
And--
Oh, mostly we have cholent.
Every Shabbos, we have cholent.
Well, it was meat, and potatoes, and beans-- mostly beans.
And my mother was-- was, you know, was baking for-- every, every Shabbos has to be baked challah, and teiglach, and strudel.
So I don't have too long my days to rest or it depends.
Somebody came to visit.
And that's how we spent Shabbos.
Then we have shaloshudes.
My mother made our own [NON-ENGLISH].
That's buttermilk we used to make.
Yeah.
Yeah.
That was, you know, typical.
I liked everything.
I liked everything.
My mother used to bake a delicious strudel from apple, apple strudel.
That was very good.
Yeah.
I just watched.
I watched my mother when she made the chicken when she come from the shochet.
I watched my mother.
And I watched her baking.
In the winter time, it was very hard because the-- the nights are long.
So my mother used to [NON-ENGLISH] the challah, like Thursday night.
And she'd get up about 3 o'clock, and she'd start to bake.
So first she made the latkes.
It was [NON-ENGLISH] latkes from buckwheat flour.
Yeah.
And were-- and some other things also.
But when we got up, the children to go to school, the latkes were already on the table.
We did tea.
And that's what my mother was [NON-ENGLISH].
Could you describe Purim?
Purim?
Oh, Purim.
Purim.
We had a yeshiva by us, you know.
The yeshiva boys used to come.
They used to make like they go to the houses and they make a play.
[SPEAKING YIDDISH] They come to the house, and they had a little corner, and they-- they'd perform.
They perform for us.
That's-- that's Purim for us.
And then, you know, we had a Purim seudat.
My mother used to bake a challah, a [NON-ENGLISH].
A [NON-ENGLISH].
It was like a half a table.
And then she makes, you know, like-- either she makes a goose probably for the seudat.
And everybody come to the Purim seudat.
All the family-- especially my mother's brothers.
They used to come to us with the family.
Purim was-- And then as I grow older, they used to make a Purim ball from the Zionist organizations.
And used to dress up in different kind of costumes, and we went out.
It was-- it was something looking forward.
Yeah.
I think it once we had like in-- from-- from the [NON-ENGLISH].
They had like a play.
[NON-ENGLISH] is like-- is a lover.
Like a lover.
[NON-ENGLISH] means love.
So afterwards, dressed up with, you know, pants and with a lot of sequins, and with crowns on the head.
Different kind of-- you know, the children.
You know, it was really-- you have to make it your own.
But I didn't know what my other friend was going to wear, so I have to be very careful.
[LAUGHS] Yeah.
Pesach.
Pesach.
[PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] My mother worked about a month before Pesach.
Everything has to be kashered.
And the kitchen has to be [NON-ENGLISH].
And [NON-ENGLISH].
We didn't have a lot of the dishes, but you have to make them-- you know, kashering the dishes.
The [NON-ENGLISH].
The [NON-ENGLISH].
And we helped.
We helped.
Yeah.
Everything has to be changed, yeah, everything.
We didn't have too much, but whatever we have, we have a lot of Pesadich dishes.
[NON-ENGLISH] that they come-- they came from the-- from the attic.
Matzah-- we baked matzah.
It was a special place that they opened up just for baking the matzah, so-- but you don't have to bring your own flour.
They bake-- you come, you order your matzah, and they bake it for you, then they bring it home.
And you push it-- you push it in a-- you put it in a special place in a corner.
It came in a big box, a [NON-ENGLISH].
And my mother-- this was already Pesadich.
Nobody even supposed to look.
And you cover it up, and then you start bringing in-- you know, the rest.
Potatoes and onions.
And-- and you didn't have matzah meal.
You didn't have farfel.
You have to do it-- you have to do it yourself.
It was a thing like-- it's called a stamp.
And you stamp it.
And this was going on to the whole family.
And then you [? sip ?] it, and you make from that farfel and matzah meal for baking.
Yeah.
Travelers?
Yeah, we have family and-- in-- in smaller towns.
Like we have family in Kostopol.
We have family in [POLISH].
We have family in [POLISH].
They used to come to visit us once in a while, yeah.
But that was short distances.
It would be about an hour with the train.
They used to come to visit us.
Before the war?
I was-- I was already finished with school, because we all go to seven-- seven grades.
It's seven-- I was about 14, 15.
And then I went to learn a-- a trade.
I learn how to sew.
A woman took me in, and she showed me how to sew.
Yeah.
I-- I was working like a year without money, and then she start paying me.
Yeah.
I was about 16 years old.
In Rowne, yeah. 16, 17, yeah.
So that's how I know how to sew.
Yeah.
Mirl.
Mirl Shmuach.
Her name was Mirl Shmuach.
Yeah.
She was a very special lady to me.
She taught me a lot.
And she had small-- two small children, a boy and a girl-- [?
Zizela. ?] And the boy's name, she used to call him [?
Engela. ?] She didn't call him by a name.
She called them [?
Zizela ?] and [?
Engela. ?] Yeah.
Not with-- not in the Polish, no.
Not by the Polack, we didn't have passports.
No.
We didn't have any telephones.
Who calls on telephone?
We didn't have radios till about 1939.
We didn't have radios.
No.
No telephone, no radios.
Maybe it was a-- a telephone in the-- in the post office, but nobody called.
No.
It changes one, two, three.
The Poles was-- in 1939, that was September 1.
Just Erev Rosh Hashanah, the war broke out between German there in Pole-- and the-- and the Poles.
So it was right-- we felt right away the-- the-- the-- the airplanes were-- right away, they were bombarding.
For three, four days, we couldn't go out.
Yeah.
So we were go-- we went to like-- like in a suburb from Rowne.
And there we stayed.
But the war didn't last too long.
It was about-- maybe 10 days, and Poland was destroyed.
Nothing. 10, maybe 12 days.
And then we were all-- then we were without-- without any government.
And then for before Yom Kippur, the Russian came to us.
Because the Russians were not far from the Polish-- from the Polish border.
I didn't know.
We didn't know what-- what Russian-- what the Soviet-- we didn't know what's what.
But at least they didn't kill us.
That was it.
Can I have a drink?
[DRINKS] They left us alone.
You know, whoever.
You know, they came.
They help-- they helped us.
Yeah.
It was right away.
It was a big change.
It was a big change.
Everything was--
Well, we didn't know, you know?
They-- they came in with the tanks, the-- the-- the Russians.
And they-- and they-- right away, they make theirselves at home.
And nothing like in the first few days.
We didn't have any bread, any nothing, because still it took them time to-- to start.
But then, we have to wait on line for bread.
You have to go out like at night.
But you know, little by little, they got-- it got better.
You got bread, you got sugar, but you have to wait on line.
Yeah.
You have to wait on line for hours.
Nothing.
Nothing much, you know.
They-- you know, maybe in the-- the higher, so some, maybe they-- but not for the average person.
It wasn't too much of a change.
So the Russian came.
They saved us.
If you wouldn't be for the Russian, probably the Germans would come.
And it didn't take them too long.
It took them another year and a-- a year and a half.
Right.
Well, it was a change, you know.
We didn't know.
We saw the-- the-- the soldiers with different uniforms, with different languages, with everything different.
But it was OK.
They didn't kill us.
Again, they did not.
Yeah.
Oh, yeah.
They closed all the businesses.
They confiscated everything.
All of a sudden, all the stores-- you know, we had a lot of stores.
Nothing.
Everything disappeared.
But it took quite a few months.
They start bringing in more goods, you know, like-- but you have to wait on line.
You know, necessities, not luxury.
Necessities-- you need a pair of shoes, you need a pair of stockings, you know.
But this-- it came, you know.
No.
My father was still a painter.
My father was still a painter.
Yeah.
My work?
I went to work-- not already like sewing, but I went to work in a store.
You know, they were supplying the-- the schools with, you know, like bakery goods.
You know, the children, they should have lunch.
So I worked in that place.
Yeah, it was OK.
Oh, we were-- not too much.
Not too much.
To tell you the truth, I-- we didn't-- I didn't know.
We didn't.
Maybe we have a lot or-- a lot of people coming from Poland.
They-- they were escaping, so they came to Rowne like [NON-ENGLISH].
So they were telling us stories what the Nazis are doing.
But in Rowne, we didn't feel-- we didn't feel it.
Well, it was very sad.
They were grabbing people from the streets and then killing them.
And this was-- I mean, this is our people.
This is our people.
This was sad, but we can do nothing.
We couldn't do nothing about it.
No.
We didn't know.
Well, the Soviet, maybe they'll-- they'll stay with us.
Maybe this is it.
So we make the best.
But they didn't take too long.
[SIGHS] And on June 22, 1941, the German broke the pact non-aggressive with the Russians, and they-- and they invaded-- they invaded Russia.
Especially our-- our place, especially Rowne.
And Wolyn-- that what you call Wolyn especially.
That was-- was invaded.
And right away, the first-- the first day, we felt the bombs.
They were bombarding like no tomorrow.
Yeah.
Rowne.
I didn't have.
Yeah.
But my father, he read the book when Hitler wrote the book.
So he know that Hitler wants to destroy the Jews.
This is no-- no escape.
So my father knew about it, yeah.
But we as young people, we didn't think about it.
So the Russians came, we had the Russian.
But we didn't think about other-- otherwise.
[PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] So that day-- that was that-- like I mentioned this-- it was constantly bombarding Rowne.
And Rowne was always like destroyed.
The only way to go was to-- towards the Russian border.
Did you see any of the Germans when they occupied?
No.
No.
No.
When we-- when we went away, we left Rowne June 25 towards the Russian-- Russian border.
And two days later, the German came to Rowne.
We didn't have-- we didn't have an arrangement.
You know, my father said, let's go.
Let's go in the suburb.
Let's go into the [NON-ENGLISH].
So we'll wait till the bomb, but then we'll come back.
But it wasn't back.
We couldn't go back because Rowne was burning from three sides.
The only way was towards the Russian border.
And that's what we start our journey.
My mother and my-- and my younger sister.
My middle sister wasn't-- at that time when the war broke out, she wasn't in Rowne.
She was working in a place.
They used to ship in films from Russia, and she was examining those films.
Quite a few young girls worked there.
So she worked there.
And she was very good, so they gave her like to go to a-- in Russian you call this a [RUSSIAN].
Like in a hotel in [NON-ENGLISH].
That was near the-- the Romanian border.
She went away on the-- on June 14.
And on June 22, the war broke out.
She couldn't come back, but she came back when the-- when the-- when the German was there.
Somehow she came back.
And a neighbor of ours-- she was hiding by-- by Christian people.
So she told us the story.
